evidence_rule: Every score must cite a specific line in the résumé. No evidence = 0 points regardless of implied skill.

criteria.name: Python Proficiency
criteria.jd_source: Python proficiency — you should be able to write a working ML script from scratch without searching StackOverflow for the basics.

scale.0: No Python experience mentioned; or only awareness of the language with zero demonstrated use.
scale.1: Completed a basic Python tutorial or course; can write simple scripts (loops, functions). No real project code.
scale.2: Wrote Python for data manipulation (Pandas/NumPy) in a project; code exists but lacks structure, tests, or type hints.
scale.3: Wrote modular, working Python across at least one non-trivial project; evidence of code quality (docstrings, type hints, or tests).
scale.4: Wrote production-quality Python across multiple projects: type hints, tests (pytest), clean module structure, code review experience.
scale.5: Demonstrated expert-level Python: shipped production code, large test suite, CI integration, and/or open-source contributions reviewed by others.

criteria.name: Machine Learning Fundamentals
criteria.jd_source: Machine learning fundamentals — supervised/unsupervised learning, model evaluation, overfitting/regularisation concepts.

scale.0: No evidence of any ML knowledge; résumé contains no ML terminology or projects.
scale.1: Completed ML courses (e.g. Andrew Ng) but no hands-on project applying the concepts.
scale.2: Completed one tutorial-based ML project; understands the basics but has not evaluated models rigorously.
scale.3: Completed end-to-end ML project with proper train/test split, metric evaluation (ROC-AUC, R², etc.); can explain overfitting.
scale.4: Multiple end-to-end ML projects with thorough evaluation; dealt with real challenges (class imbalance, hyperparameter search, iterative improvement).
scale.5: Demonstrates deep ML understanding: handles tricky real-world data, iterates based on error analysis, documents insights — not just final accuracy.

criteria.name: End-to-End ML/AI Project
criteria.jd_source: At least one real ML/AI project completed end-to-end (data → model → output), not just a tutorial follow-along.

scale.0: No project evidence; all ML exposure is theoretical or from watching videos.
scale.1: Completed a tutorial project step-by-step; followed instructions rather than making independent design choices.
scale.2: Built a project end-to-end but it stays local/notebook; no deployment, no write-up, no evidence of independent decision-making.
scale.3: Built an end-to-end project with independent decisions (dataset choice, architecture, evaluation approach); has some documentation or presentation.
scale.4: Built and deployed/published an end-to-end project; has clear documentation, thoughtful design decisions, and user-facing output.
scale.5: Multiple deployed end-to-end projects; each demonstrates problem framing, architecture decisions, real evaluation, and clear communication of results.

criteria.name: LLM / Agentic AI Familiarity
criteria.jd_source: Familiarity with LLMs: knows what prompt engineering means, has used at least one LLM API.

scale.0: Has never used an LLM API; only knows LLMs exist as a concept.
scale.1: Used ChatGPT or similar as a chatbot; knows what prompt engineering means conceptually but has not written prompts in code.
scale.2: Made basic API calls (OpenAI Chat Completions); wrote a simple Q&A script. No prompt engineering beyond system prompt.
scale.3: Built a functional LLM-integrated feature: RAG pipeline, tool-calling, or multi-turn chatbot with proper prompt design and output parsing.
scale.4: Built multiple LLM-integrated systems; has done prompt evaluation (RAGAS or similar), understands retrieval, chunking, embedding.
scale.5: Deep LLM engineering experience: agentic systems, LangGraph/CrewAI, guardrails, evals, production LLM integration with observability.

criteria.name: Collaboration & Communication
criteria.jd_source: Version control (Git), participate in code review, document design decisions, present results clearly to non-technical stakeholders. Collaborate in 2–3 person teams.

scale.0: No evidence of working in a team or using version control; no documentation or presentation experience.
scale.1: Has used Git for personal repos; no team collaboration, code review, or documented results.
scale.2: Basic team experience; used Git but no PR/code review; presented results informally or to peers only.
scale.3: Worked in a team using Git/PRs; has presented results to stakeholders (even in an academic/intern setting); some written documentation.
scale.4: Strong evidence of team collaboration (PRs, code review); clear technical writing; effective presentations to non-technical audiences.
scale.5: Exemplary collaboration: code review history, published technical writing (blog/paper), consistently communicates complex results to mixed audiences.

notes: Scores are computed as: weighted_total = sum(criterion.weight * criterion.score) for all criteria.
The fairness rule: name, gender, age, college prestige are NEVER criteria. Only the five JD-derived criteria above count.
The evidence rule is absolute: a score > 0 requires citing a specific résumé line or section.
